title: Improvements in or relating to Sintered Tool Tips
abstract: 1,166,839. Sintered cemented carbides. ASSOCIATED ELECTRICAL INDUSTRIES Ltd. Dec.18, 1967 [Dec.21, 1966], No.57233/66. Heading C7D. A composite sintered cemented carbide tool tip material is made from at least two different grades of powder mixtures each consisting of one or more refractory metal carbides such as WC, TiC, TaC, NbC, and one of the binders Fe, Co or Ni, one of the mixtures (A) being compacted and partially sintered and then crushed to form granules which are then mixed with the other powder mixture (B) to form a composite mixture which is compacted and sintered to form a composite material consisting of a sintered matrix of the powder mixture (B) containing dispersed sintered granules of the mixture (A) previously partially sintered, in the size range 50-250 microns. Examples are given of the preparation of the following composites made from two different grades of mixtures, one of which is a mixture of W% of an 80/20 of WC and TiC with X% Co, and the other is a mixture of Y% WC and Z% Co, where W, X, Y and Z are respectively 5-50%, 3-10%, 5-50% and 3-50% by total weight of the final sintered composite. The composite can be used for machining steel or cast iron workpieces at a surface speed of 600 ft. /minute at a feedrate 0??020 inches/revolution of the workpiece. The metal carbides have a grain size of 1-5 microns and the method can be such as to produce granules of a cast iron cutting grade in a matrix of a steel cutting grade or vice versa. Alternatively two steel cutting grades may be used together or two cast iron cutting grades may be combined to form a matrix of coarse grained cemented carbide with a dispersion of a fine grained cemented carbide.
description:
PATENT SPECIFICATION
DRAWINGS ATTACHED Tnventors: HARVEY FRUISH and JOHN RANDELL Date of filing Complete Specification: 18 Dec., 1967.
Application Date: 21 Dec., 1966.
Complete Specification Published: 8 Oct., 1969.
Index at acceptance: -C7 D(8J, 8M, 8R, 8U, 8W, 8Y, 8Z5, Al) International Classification: -B 22 f 1/00 COMPLETE SPECIFICATION
Improvements in or relating to Sintered Tool Tips We, ASSOCIATED ELECTRICAL INDUSTRIES LIMITED, a British Company having its registered office at 33, Grosvenor Place, London, S.W.l, do hereby declare the invention, for which we pray that a patent may be granted to us, and the method by which it is to be performed, to be particularly described in and by the following statement: This invention is concerned with improvements in or relating to sintered tool tips formed of cemented refractory metal carbides, and is concerned particularly but not exclusively with such tool tips intended to be used in or for or as cutting tools for machining or otherwise cutting metal. The expression "sintered tool tip" is used herein to refer to a tool tip which is made by sintering suitably prepared constituents to form a material for the toop tip, which material will be referred to herein as "sintered tool-tip material".
Known sintered tool-tip material composed of cemented refractory metal carbides is available in a number of different forms which will be referred to herein as "grades", a particular grade being particularly suitable for a particular cutting operation with the tool tip concerned, e.g. for cutting a particular metal and/or for cutting metal in a particular way. Generally speaking, when carrying out two different such cutting operations, it is necessary to employ two different tool tips made respectively of two suitable different grades of sintered tool-tip material.
It is an object of the invention to provide a composite sintered tool-tip material which consists of at least two such sintered tooltip materials of different grades, whereby a sintered tool tip made of such a material can be used to perform at least two different [Price 4s. 6d.] cutting operations for which at least two different sintered tool tips would normally be required.
According to the first aspect of the invention, there is provided a sintered tool tip which is formed of at least two sintered tool tip materials of different grades as hereinafter defined, each said material being composed of at least one refractory metal carbide together with a binder which is one of the metals iron, nickel or cobalt, and which tool tip consists of a matrix composed of a said material of one grade with, substantially uniformly dispersed throughout the matrix, at least one said material of another grade in the form of a multiplicity of aggregations each of dimensions lying in the range of 50 to 250 microns.
It will be understood that by the expression "sintered tool tip materials of different grades" as used herein is meant such materials of different compositions in respect of the refractory metal carbide and/or binder metal constituents or relative proportions of the constituents present, and having different properties such that material of each grade is particularly suitable for use for a specific type of cutting operation (as exemplified above) which is distinct from the type of cutting operation for which material of any other grade is suitable.
It will also be understood that the term 'aggregations" used herein to describe the disperse phase or phases in the sintered tool tip means relatively large discrete particles, in the size range indicated, each formed of a sintered coherent mass of refractory metal carbide particles and binder metal and having the desired composition of a sintered tool tip material of a particular grade as aforesaid.
1 <166,839 No. 57233/66.
According to the second aspect of the in.
vention, a method of manufacturing a composite sintered tool tip material includes the steps of separately preparing at least tw different powder mixtures each consisting o:
one or more refractory metal carbides and onc of the binder metals iron, nickel or cobalt, in the required relative proportions, for forming at least two sintered tool tip materials ol different grades as hereinbefore defined, reserving one said mixture, separately compacting and partially sintering the other, or each other, said mixture and breaking down the partially sintered material or materials into granules of agglomerate form, mixing the granules with the said reserved powder mixture, and compacting and sintering the resulting mixture to form a composite material consisting of a sintered matrix of material of one grade formed from the said reserved powder mixture and, substantially uniformly dispersed throughout the matrix, a multiplicity of aggregations, as hereinbefore defined, of a material or materials of a different grade or grades formed from the said granules, the partially sintered granules being of such sizes that the aggregations formed on final sintering thereof are each of dimensions lying in the range of 50 to 250 microns.
Preferably the aggregations have dimensions lying in the range of 75 to 105 microns, the partially sintered material or materials being broken down in such a manner as to produce agglomerate granules of dimensions to give aggregations in this range on final sintering.
The invention also includes a sintered tool tip according to the invention, when the tip is in or for a cutting tool for machining or otherwise cutting metal, and the invention also includes such a cutting tool having a sintered tool tip according to the invention.
The invention will now be described, by way of example, with reference to the drawings accompanying the provisional specification, in which:
Figure 1 is a perspective drawing of one end only of a tipped tool suitable for the machining of metal; and Figure 2 is a diagrammatic representation of the structure of a composite sintered tooltip material in accordance with the invention.
Referring first to Figure 1, the tool comprises a shank 1 of steel formed at one end with a recess 3 into which is secured in any suitable manner a hard tool tip 5. The tip 5 is in the form of a thin flat plate, and in use the tip edge 7 is the cutting edge of the tool. The flat face 9 of the tool tip forms the rake face of the tool, and the edge 11 of the tool tip forms the relief face or flank. As swarf is cut from the workpiece, it passes over the rake face 9 of the tool while the freshly cut workpiece surface moves past the relief face 11.
In use of such a tipped tool, swarf moving over the rake face 9 tends to cause a crater 13 to form close to the cutting edge, while the relief face 11 tends to wear away at 15.
In addition to wear caused by attrition, additional wear on the rake face and on the relief face may be caused by diffusion of metal carbide from the material of the tool tip 5 into the rotating workpiece. When steel is cut at high speed, high temperatures -are generated in the tool tip 5, and the rate of such diffusion is then relatively high. A further effect of the increase in the tool tip temperature is to increase the deformation of the tool cutting edge 7 under the action of the cutting forces.
The tips used in tools of this general type are made of a sintered mixture essentially of a metal carbide such as tungsten carbide and a metal binder of the iron group. The most commonly used binder metal is cobalt. Where tungsten carbide is used, the tool tip is particularly suited to the cutting of cast iron and non-ferrous metals, but shows a high rate of cratering when used for cutting steel. To improve the resistance to cratering when cutting steel, some of the tungsten carbide can be replaced by one or more of the carbides of elements in the 4th and 5th groups of the Periodic Table. Titanium carbide reduces cratering, but embrittles the tool tip. If tantalum carbide is added as well, the tool becomes tougher and more resistant to chipping and plastic deformation. Such sintered mixtures containing titanium carbide and/or tantalum carbide are commonly known as "steel cutting grades", and three typical examples are (by weight):
Example A:
86% Tungsten Carbide 5% Titanium Carbide 9% Cobalt Example B:
81% Tungsten Carbide 12% Titanium Carbide 7% Cobalt Example C:
73% Tungsten Carbide 10% Titanium Carbide 8% Tantalum Carbide 9% Cobalt Commercial tantalum carbide commonly contains a substantial proportion of niobium carbide, for example 25%, and any reference to tantalum carbide is always understood as including whatever niobium carbide is present.
Although tantalum carbide makes the tool tip tougher, titanium carbide is several times as effective, weight for weight, as tantalum carbide in imparting wear resistance to the tip.
The presence of titanium carbide and/or 1,166,839 Steel cutting content:
80/20 (by weight) Tungsten Carbide/ Titanium Carbide mixture 47 % by total weight Cobalt 3% by total weight Cast iron cutting content:
Tungsten Carbide 47% by total weight Cobalt 3% by total weight More generally, composite materials designed to give good results both when machining cast iron and when machining steel may have compositions of the forms:Steel cutting content:
80/20 (by weight) Tungsten Carbide/ Titanium Carbide mixture 5-50% by total weight Cobalt 3-10% by total weight Cast iron cutting content:
Tungsten carbide 5-50% by total weight Cobalt 3-50% by total weight The grain size of the metal carbides used in producing sintered tool tips lies in the range 1 to 5 microns. It is desired to produce aggregations of the cast iron cutting content surrounded and contained by a matrix of the steel cutting content or, conversely, aggregations of the steel cutting content surrounded and contained by a matrix of the cast iron cutting content, the aggregations having dimensions lying in the range 50 to 250 microns and preferably lying in the range to 105 microns. To achieve the former result, first the constituents of the desired cast iron cutting material are mixed and compacted and lightly or partially sintered to produce a coherent mass which does not have the full strength of such a material when prepared as a sintered tool tip. This mass is then crushed and broken down to granules each of agglomerate form (i.e., each granule is a granular agglomerate, an approximately ball-shaped cohesive mass of the mixed constituents) and having dimensions lying in the upper end of the desired size range of the aggregations to be produced. These granules are then mixed with the powdered constituents of the steel cutting grade material, and the whole compacted and sintered to produce the desired tool tip. In the finished material, each of the granules of cast iron cutting content forms one of the desired aggregations, some reduction in its size taking place during the final compacting and sintering stages.
Another example of the practical use of the invention is the combination in a tool tip of two steel cutting grades in the form of aggregations of a brittle, high wear resistance grade in a matrix of a tough high impact resistance grade. This produces a tool combining the properties of both grades, and tantalum carbide in the tool tip greatly reduces cratering when cutting steel at high speed. It also reduces wear of the relief face, although to a lesser degree, since the solubility of these carbides in steel and iron at high temperatures is lower than that of tungsten carbide. However, the presence of titanium carbide and/or tantalum carbide has three disadvantages for applications other than high speed cutting of steel. Firstly, when wear is caused mainly by attrition, that is by the tearing away of small fragments of the tip material, as when cutting cast iron and as when cutting steel at low speeds, the rate of wear of the relief face is increased.
Secondly, the thermal conductivity of the tip is decreased, and this tends to retain the heat generated in the part of the tip adjacent the cutting edge, so increasing the rate of diffu2C sion and increasing the deformation of the cutting edge. Thirdly, the cutting edge strength of the tool tip is reduced, and this may lead to fracture by mechanical shock during roughing cuts, and also to the development of cracks by thermal shock caused by the rapid heating and cooling of the tool as it enters and leaves the workpiece. For cutting cast iron and non-ferrous metals, a tip material consisting of tungsten carbide and 4 to 12% by weight of cobalt is commonly used because of its resistance to attrition.
It will be seen that for different types of operation different compositions of tool tip material are required for optimum results, and it is common practice to manufacture separate categories of the said sintered mixtures to cater for the machining of cast iron and for the machining of steel. Within each main category there are many different compositions to cover the range of workpiece materials and machining conditions. In general, a particular "grade" is selected for a particular cuting operation.
A sintered tool tip in accordance with the present invention, which is formed of a matrix of sintered tool tip material of one grade with, dispersed therein, aggregations of sintered tool tip material or materials of one or more different other grades, can be used for two or more different cutting operations.
For example, where there are only two grades, one can be a grade of material particularly suitable for machining cast iron, and the other a grade of material particularly suitable for machining steel, and the tip is then usable at will on either cast iron or steel. The following material is an example of a composite material designed to give good result both when machining cast iron and when machining steel at a surface speed of 600 feet per minute and at a feed rate of 0.020 inch per revolution of the workpiece.
1,166,839 1,166,839 equally suitable for making roughing cuts, when the tool is in intermittent contact with the workpiece so setting up severe mechanical and thermal shocks, and for the rapid removal of metal when the tool is in continuous contact with the workpiece.
Still another example is the use for machining of cast iron of a fine-grained grade of material, suitable for high speed machining, in the form of aggregations contained in a tough, coarse-grained matrix suitable for slower machining with a heavier cut.
In general, the properties of the material used to form the aggregations are not seriously affected by the material used to forn the matrix, and the properties of the material used to form the matrix are not seriously affected by the material used to form the aggregations. Although such a composite tool tip cannot necessarily be expected to be as good in meeting the requirements of a particular machining task as a tool tip prepared specifically to meet that task, it is found that the composite tool tip does have a performance much better than that of an ordinary tip which is being used not for its intended use but for an alternative use to which it is not really suited.
By the use of composite tips according to the present invention, the number of different tips it is necessary to stock is considerably reduced, and what is more important the need to change the tool, even on a single workpiece, when changing from one cut to another, and particularly when changing from one work-piece of steel to another of cast iron, can often be avoided. From the practical and economic point of view, the consequent saving in time on a production lina using expensive machinery is important.
claims:
WHAT WE CLAIM IS: -
1. A sintered tool tip which is formed of at least two sintered tool tip materials of different grades as hereinbefore defined, each said material being composed of at least one refractory metal carbide together with a binder which is one of the metals iron, nickel or cobalt, and which tool tip consists of a matrix composed of a said material of one grade with, substantially uniformly dispersed throughout the matrix, at least one said material of another grade in the form of a multiplicity of aggregations, as hereinbefore defined, each of dimensions lying in the range of 50 to 250 microns.
2. A sintered tool tip according to Claim 1, wherein all the said aggregations are formed of material of the same said other grade.
3. A sintered tool tip according to Claim 1 or 2, wherein the said aggregations have dimensions lying in the range of 75 to 105 microns.
4. A sintered tool tip according to Claim 1, 2 or 3, wherein the or at least one of the 65 refractory metal carbides is tungsten carbide.
5. A sintered tool tip according to any preceding Claim, wherein there are at least two of the metal carbides at least one of which is a carbide of an element in the 4th 70 or 5th group of the Periodic Table.
6. A sintered tool tip according to Claim 5, wherein the said element is titanium.
7. A sintered tool tip according to Claim 5, wherein the said element is tantalum. 75 8. A sintered tool tip according to Claim 7, wherein the tantalum carbide is commercially available tantalum carbide and contains a proportion of niobium carbide.
9. A sintered tool tip according to any 80 preceding Claim, wherein the said metal of the iron group is cobalt.
10. A sintered tool tip according to any preceding Claim, wherein one of the said materials is made of a sintered mixture of 85 tungsten carbide and 4 to 12% by weight of cobalt.
11. A sintered tool tip according to Claim 1, wherein there are only two of the said materials of which one is made of a sintered 90 mixture of (w)% of an 80/20 mixture (by weight) of tungsten carbide and titanium carbide with (x)% of cobalt, and of which the other is made of a sintered mixture of (y)% of tungsten carbide and (z)% of cobalt, 95 where (w), (x), (y) and (i) lie respectively in the ranges 5-50%, 3-10%, 5-50% and 3-50% by total weight of the final tooltip material.
12. A sintered tool tip according to Claim 100 11, wherein (w), (x), (y) and (z) are respectively 47%, 3%, 47% and 3%, by total weight of the final tool tip material.
13. A method of manufacturing a composite sintered tool tip material which includes the 105 steps of separately preparing at least two different powder mixtures each consisting of one or more refractory metal carbides and one of the binder metals iron, nickel or cobalt, in the required relative proportions, 110 for forming at least two sintered tool tip materials of different grades as hereinbefore defined, reserving one said mixture, separately compacting and partially sintering the other, or each other, said mixture and breaking 115 down the partially sintered material or materials into granules of agglomerate form, mixing the granules with the said reserved powder mixture, and compacting and sintering the resulting mixture to form a composite 120 material consisting of a sintered matrix of material of one grade formed from the said reserved powder mixture and, substantially uniformly dispersed throughout the matrix, a multiplicity of aggregations, as hereinbefore 125 defined, of a material or materials of a different grade or grades formed from the said granules, the partially sintered granules being of such sizes that the aggregations formed on 1,166,839 final sintering thereof are each of dimensions lying in the range of 50 to 250 microns.
14. A method according to Claim 13, wherein only two of the said powder mixtures, corresponding respectively to materials of two of the said grades, are employed.
15. A method according to Claim 13 or 14, wherein the dimensions of the granules produced by breaking down the partially sintered material or materials are so selected that the corresponding aggregations formed therefrom have dimensions lying in the range of 75 to 105 microns.
16. A method according to Claim 13, 14 or 15, wherein the or at least one of the metal carbides is tungsten carbide.
17. A method according to Claim 13, 14, or 16, wherein there are at least two of the metal carbides at least one of which is a carbide of an element in the 4th or 5th group of the Periodic Table.
18. A method according to Claim 17, wherein the said element is titanium.
19. A method according to Claim 17, wherein the said element is tantalum.
20. A method according to Claim 19, wherein the tantalum carbide is commercially available tantalum carbide and contains a proportion of niobium carbide.
21. A method according to any one of Claims 13 to 20, wherein the said metal of the iron group is cobalt.
22. A method according to any one of Claims 13 to 21, wherein the constituents of one said powder mixture are tungsten carbide and 4 to 12% by weight of cobalt.
23. A method according to Claim 13, wherein there are only two of the said powder mixtures, the constituents of one said mixture being (w)% of an 80/20 mixture (by weight) of tungsten carbide and titanium carbide with (x)% of cobalt, and the constituents of the other said mixture being (y)% of tungsten carbide and (z)% of cobalt, where (w), (x), (y) and (z) lie respectively in the ranges 5-50%, 3-10%, 5-50% and 3-50% by total weight of the composite sintered tool-tip material.
24. A method according to Claim 23, wherein (w), (x), (y) and (z) are respectively 47%, 3%, 47% and 3%, by total weight of the composite sintered tool tip material.
25. A sintered tool tip according to any one of Claims 1-12, wherein at least one of the said grades is particularly suitable for the cutting of a particular metal.
26. A sintered tool tip according to any one of Claims 1-12 and 25, wherein at least one of the said grades is particularly suitable for the cutting of a metal in a particular way.
27. A sintered tool tip according to Claim 26, wherein the said particular way is the cutting of the metal at a particular speed and/or the feeding of the sintered tool tip to a workpiece at a particular rate and/or in a particular manner.
28. A sintered tool tip substantially as specifically described herein with reference to the drawings accompanying the provisional specification.
29. In or for a cutting tool for machining or otherwise cutting metal, a sintered tool tip according to any one of Claims 1-12 and 25-28.
30. A cutting tool for machining or otherwise cutting metal, the tool having a sintered tool tip according to any one of Claims 1-12 and 25-28.
